trajectory_summary: The call starts cold, moves Quanisha from 'not right now' to 'possibly next week,' and surfaces a genuine curiosity signal at 04:54 — but Carter never converts any of the soft commitments into a locked appointment. The call ends with two open asks (bill and time), no confirmed sit, and the prior-disqualification concern unaddressed. Energy was built in the middle of the call and then leaked at the close.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but based on the transcript the call should be tagged as 'tentative — no appointment booked.' Carter explicitly defers scheduling ('just let me know a time that works') rather than locking a time. This is a soft callback, not a booked sit, and should not be counted as a sit for compensation purposes per the setter comp model.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Button-up discipline — converting soft callbacks to locked sits
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Just send me that utility bill as soon as you can. And then if you could also just let me know a time that works, that'd be great.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Carter moved Quanisha from 'not right now' to 'possibly next week' — that's real work. But he exited without a locked time, a confirmed fiance on the appointment, or any of the button_up protocol elements. The call ends as a soft callback with two open asks, which is the highest no-show risk exit available. Per the comp model, this call doesn't pay until it sits. Manager should walk Carter through the button_up protocol and have him practice the two-option close ('Does Monday at 10 or Tuesday at 2 work for both of you?') plus the 'is that fair' handshake.

key_moments.quote: Oh, you didn't qualify for it?
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter surfaces the prior-disqualification concern but doesn't resolve it — he asks a clarifying question, gets an uncertain answer ('I'm not quite sure'), and then pivots to a pitch. The unresolved 'why didn't I qualify' is the customer's primary anxiety and it never gets addressed. Reactive read: Carter heard the concern and moved past it. Strategic read: Carter was deferring resolution to the closer. The reactive read is more supported — Carter's next move is a generic pitch, not a setup for a later resolution.

key_moments.quote: there is a tax credit in place right now that's gonna be leaving at the end of the year.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter deploys the tax-credit urgency lever before he knows whether the customer qualifies for it. The customer's fiance is the title-holder and the credit qualifier hasn't been confirmed yet. Using urgency on a customer who may not qualify for the incentive being cited is a credibility risk — if the closer later has to walk back the tax credit, the handoff is damaged. This is a reactive move: Carter reached for the nearest urgency tool without checking whether it applied.

key_moments.quote: their upfront cost is just gonna be eliminated completely, and then we'd swap it from your utility bill. Looks like from what I see, it's your West Southside Electric. So we'd swap that, your expensive bill, to a cheaper fixated solar bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: Carter explains the financial mechanism of solar — upfront cost elimination, bill swap — before the customer asked. This is setter-scope creep: the setter's job is to create curiosity and schedule the closer, not to explain how the product works financially. The explanation is accurate but premature. It also partially answers the payment question Quanisha asks at 04:54, which means Carter gave away closer-tier content for free and then had to repeat it when she asked anyway.

key_moments.quote: my fiance name is on it. Mine is not at at this time.
key_moments.stewart_read: Quanisha discloses that her fiance is the title-holder, not her. Carter acknowledges this ('Okay. So your fiance is the owner, though') and moves on without addressing the scheduling implication. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the title-holder must be on the appointment. Carter never asks whether the fiance will be available, never confirms the fiance's name, and never anchors the appointment around both of them being present. The call ends with a soft scheduling ask directed at Quanisha alone ('are you both gonna be around on Monday'), which is the only gesture toward fiance inclusion — and it's too late and too soft.
